revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: ''''无位'''（'''無位'''・むい）とは、[[律令制]]において[[位階]]を持っていない者。特に[[官司]]に仕えて[[官職]]に在職...'
revision.text:
'''无位'''（'''無位'''・むい）とは、[[律令制]]において[[位階]]を持っていない者。特に[[官司]]に仕えて[[官職]]に在職しているにも関わらず、位階を有していない者を指す場合がある。
== 概要 == 律令制における官職の基本は、官職と位階が対応する[[官位相当制]]を採用していた（[[令外官]]の場合、官位相当制が採用されていない官職もある）。だが、[[四等官]]よりも下の官職でかつ[[品官]]の扱いを受けていない官職の中には官位相当を持たない[[雑任]]と呼ばれる下級官人が多く存在した。
例えば、[[神祇官]]の定員総数は89名であるが官位相当を有する職事官は7名、東西の[[市司]]はそれぞれ39名の定員を有していたが職事官は正・佑・令史の3名で価長以下は全員官位相当を有しない雑任であった。こうした雑任の中には初位・八位などの低い位階を持つ者だけでなく、无位であった者も含まれていた。
実際に[[養老律令]]などの規定を見ると、无位の官人の存在を前提とした規定が確認できる。例えば、[[衣服令]]には无位の服装は黄袍と規定され、[[雑令]]には文武官の義務として毎年正月15日に行う薪の進納規定の中で无位は1担と定められている。
こうした无位の官人も考叙の手続に沿って雑任などに[[補任]]され、彼らは年功や[[労]]を積み重ねて、昇進や位階獲得の機会を伺い、中には[[考課]]を経て[[叙位]]の機会に恵まれる者もいたがそれは一部の者であったとみられている。だが、その実態については不明な部分が多い。
== 参考文献 == *梅村喬「古代官職制と〈職〉」『「職」成立過程の研究』（校倉書房、2011年） ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7
{{DEFAULTSORT:むい}} [[Category:日本の律令制]]

'''无位'''（'''無位'''・むい）とは、[[律令制]]において[[位階]]を持っていない者。特に[[官司]]に仕えて[[官職]]に在職しているにもかかわらず、位階を有していない者を指す場合がある。

実際に[[養老律令]]などの規定を見ると、无位の官人の存在を前提とした規定が確認できる。例えば、[[衣服令]]には无位の服装は黄袍と規定され、[[雑令]]には文武官の義務として毎年正月15日に行う薪の進納規定の中で无位は1担と定められている。
こうした无位の官人も考叙の手続に沿って雑任などに[[補任]]され、彼らは年功や[[労]]を積み重ねて、昇進や位階獲得の機会を伺い、中には[[考課]]を経て[[叙位]]の機会に恵まれる者もいたがそれは一部の者であったとみられている。だが、その実態については不明な部分が多い。

revision.text:
'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位・むい) refers to individuals without an official rank (位階) under the [[Ritsuryō system]]. This term often specifically denotes those serving in [[government offices]] and holding [[official positions]] but lacking any formal rank.
== Overview ==
Under the Ritsuryō system, official positions were generally governed by the [[Kan'i Sōtō System]] (官位相当制), which aligned positions with corresponding ranks. However, in some cases, such as for [[Ryōgaikan]] (令外官), the Kan'i Sōtō System was not applied. Many lower-ranking officials, particularly those below the [[Shitōkan]] (四等官) level and not classified as [[Hinkan]] (品官), held positions without corresponding ranks. These individuals were classified as [[Zatsunin]] (雑任), lower-tier officials without official rank.
For example: - The [[Jingikan]] had 89 total positions, but only 7 were ranked officials under the Kan'i Sōtō System. - The [[market offices]] (東市司 and 西市司) had 39 positions each, but only 3 officials (正, 佑, 令史) had ranks, while the remaining officials (below Kachō) were all unranked Zatsunin.
Among these Zatsunin were individuals with lower ranks, such as [[Shōi]] (初位) or [[Hachii]] (八位), as well as those classified as Mu-i.
Provisions within the [[Yōrō Code]] (養老律令) acknowledge the existence of unranked officials. For instance: - The [[Clothing Code]] (衣服令) prescribed yellow robes (黄袍) for Mu-i individuals. - The [[Zatsu Code]] (雑令) outlined obligations for civil and military officials, including the requirement for Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood annually on the 15th day of the first month.
These unranked officials were appointed to Zatsunin roles following [[Kōjo]] (考叙) procedures. Through years of service and accumulation of experience or merit ([[Rō]]), some eventually advanced or acquired ranks through the [[Kōka]] (考課) system. However, such opportunities were limited, and much about the lives and roles of Mu-i individuals remains unclear.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Umemura Takashi|title=Ancient Office Systems and "Shoku"|chapter=The Process of Establishing "Shoku"|year=2011|publisher=Kōzō Shoin|isbn=978-4-7517-4360-7}}
{{DEFAULTSORT:むい}} [[Category:Ritsuryō system in Japan]] [[Category:Rank system in Japan]]

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位・むい) refers to individuals without an official rank (位階) under the [[Ritsuryō system]]. This term often specifically denotes those serving in [[government offices]] and holding [[official positions]] but lacking any formal rank.
== Overview ==
Under the Ritsuryō system, official positions were generally governed by the [[Kan'i Sōtō System]] (官位相当制), which aligned positions with corresponding ranks. However, in some cases, such as for [[Ryōgaikan]] (令外官), the Kan'i Sōtō System was not applied. Many lower-ranking officials, particularly those below the [[Shitōkan]] (四等官) level and not classified as [[Hinkan]] (品官), held positions without corresponding ranks. These individuals were classified as [[Zatsunin]] (雑任), lower-tier officials without official rank.
For example: - The [[Jingikan]] had 89 total positions, but only 7 were ranked officials under the Kan'i Sōtō System. - The [[market offices]] (東市司 and 西市司) had 39 positions each, but only 3 officials (正, 佑, 令史) had ranks, while the remaining officials (below Kachō) were all unranked Zatsunin.
Among these Zatsunin were individuals with lower ranks, such as [[Shōi]] (初位) or [[Hachii]] (八位), as well as those classified as Mu-i.
Provisions within the [[Yōrō Code]] (養老律令) acknowledge the existence of unranked officials. For instance: - The [[Clothing Code]] (衣服令) prescribed yellow robes (黄袍) for Mu-i individuals. - The [[Zatsu Code]] (雑令) outlined obligations for civil and military officials, including the requirement for Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood annually on the 15th day of the first month.
These unranked officials were appointed to Zatsunin roles following [[Kōjo]] (考叙) procedures. Through years of service and accumulation of experience or merit ([[Rō]]), some eventually advanced or acquired ranks through the [[Kōka]] (考課) system. However, such opportunities were limited, and much about the lives and roles of Mu-i individuals remains unclear.

{{DEFAULTSORT:むい}} [[Category:Ritsuryō system in Japan]] [[Category:Rank system in Japan]] [[Category:New pages]][[ja:无位]]

== Overview ==
Under the Ritsuryō system, official positions were generally governed by the [[Kan'i Sōtō System]] (官位相当制), which aligned positions with corresponding ranks. However, in some cases, such as for [[Ryōgaikan]] (令外官), the Kan'i Sōtō System was not applied. Many lower-ranking officials, particularly those below the [[Shitōkan]] (四等官) level and not classified as [[Hinkan]] (品官), held positions without corresponding ranks. These individuals were classified as [[Zatsunin]] (雑任), lower-tier officials without official rank.
For example: - The [[Jingikan]] had 89 total positions, but only 7 were ranked officials under the Kan'i Sōtō System. - The [[market offices]] (東市司 and 西市司) had 39 positions each, but only 3 officials (正, 佑, 令史) had ranks, while the remaining officials (below Kachō) were all unranked Zatsunin.
Among these Zatsunin were individuals with lower ranks, such as [[Shōi]] (初位) or [[Hachii]] (八位), as well as those classified as Mu-i.
Provisions within the [[Yōrō Code]] (養老律令) acknowledge the existence of unranked officials. For instance: - The [[Clothing Code]] (衣服令) prescribed yellow robes (黄袍) for Mu-i individuals. - The [[Zatsu Code]] (雑令) outlined obligations for civil and military officials, including the requirement for Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood annually on the 15th day of the first month.
These unranked officials were appointed to Zatsunin roles following [[Kōjo]] (考叙) procedures. Through years of service and accumulation of experience or merit ([[Rō]]), some eventually advanced or acquired ranks through the [[Kōka]] (考課) system. However, such opportunities were limited, and much about the lives and roles of Mu-i individuals remains unclear.
== References == * {{Cite book|author=Umemura Takashi|title=Ancient Office Systems and "Shoku"|chapter=The Process of Establishing "Shoku"|year=2011|publisher=Kōzō Shoin|isbn=978-4-7517-4360-7}}

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位・むい) refers to individuals without an official rank (位階) under the [[Ritsuryō system]]. This term often specifically denotes those serving in [[government offices]] and holding [[official positions]] but lacking any formal rank.
== Overview ==
Under the Ritsuryō system, official positions were generally governed by the [[Kan'i Sōtō System]] (官位相当制), which aligned positions with corresponding ranks. However, in some cases, such as for [[Ryōgaikan]] (令外官), the Kan'i Sōtō System was not applied. Many lower-ranking officials, particularly those below the [[Shitōkan]] (四等官) level and not classified as [[Hinkan]] (品官), held positions without corresponding ranks. These individuals were classified as [[Zatsunin]] (雑任), lower-tier officials without official rank.
For example: - The [[Jingikan]] had 89 total positions, but only 7 were ranked officials under the Kan'i Sōtō System. - The [[market offices]] (東市司 and 西市司) had 39 positions each, but only 3 officials (正, 佑, 令史) had ranks, while the remaining officials (below Kachō) were all unranked Zatsunin.
Among these Zatsunin were individuals with lower ranks, such as [[Shōi]] (初位) or [[Hachii]] (八位), as well as those classified as Mu-i.
Provisions within the [[Yōrō Code]] (養老律令) acknowledge the existence of unranked officials. For instance: - The [[Clothing Code]] (衣服令) prescribed yellow robes (黄袍) for Mu-i individuals. - The [[Zatsu Code]] (雑令) outlined obligations for civil and military officials, including the requirement for Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood annually on the 15th day of the first month.
These unranked officials were appointed to Zatsunin roles following [[Kōjo]] (考叙) procedures. Through years of service and accumulation of experience or merit ([[Rō]]), some eventually advanced or acquired ranks through the [[Kōka]] (考課) system. However, such opportunities were limited, and much about the lives and roles of Mu-i individuals remains unclear.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
[[Category:Ritsuryō system of Japan]] [[Category:Rank system in Japan]] [[ja:无位]]

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.

While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court rank|ja|位階}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職}} but did not possess a court rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制}}, the correlation between official positions and court ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官}} with corresponding ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任}} without corresponding ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位}}, but also individuals entirely without rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court Rank|ja|位階|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職|qq=draft|qq=draft}} but did not possess a court Rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the correlation between official positions and court Ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|qq=draft}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|qq=draft}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
{{translated page|ja|无位|version=86401846|comment=Imported full ja history}}

'''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court Rank|ja|位階|qq=draft|qq=draft}} under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職|qq=draft|qq=draft}} but did not possess a court Rank.
== Overview == Under the {{ill|Ritsuryō system|ja|律令制|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, the correlation between official positions and court Ranks was defined by the {{ill|Rank-Position Correspondence System|ja|官位相当制|1=Kan'i sōtō system|qq=draft|qq=draft}}. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and those not treated as {{ill|Pinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.
For example: - The {{ill|Department of Divinities|ja|神祇官|1=Department of Divinities|blank=|qq=draft|qq=draft}} had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|qq=draft|qq=draft}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|qq=draft|qq=draft}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|qq=draft}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the {{ill|Yōrō Code|ja|養老律令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|qq=draft}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|qq=}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|qq=}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Umemura|qq=}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier System of Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} and those not treated as {{ill|Hinkan|lt=Hinkan|lt=Pinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pinkan|qq=}}.
For example: - The [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikiji Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 職事}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|qq=}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大初位}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachi-i|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Umemura|qq=}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
{{translated page|ja|无位|version=86401846|comment=Imported full ja history}}

[[Category:Completion tier 3]] [[Category:Ritsuryō system in Japan]] }}

For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.

However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier System of Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} and those not treated as {{ill|Hinkan|lt=Hinkan|lt=Pinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pinkan|qq=}}.
For example: - The [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikiji Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 職事|1=Shikiji Officials|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|qq=}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大初位|1=Greater Initial Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433041}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachi-i|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位|1=Senior Eighth Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545368}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.

This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Executive (government)|qq=|1=kanshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452091}} and held {{ill|Kanshoku|lt=official positions|ja|官職|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanshoku|qq=}} but did not possess a court Rank.

However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, were excluded from this system. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōi|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大初位|1=Greater Initial Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433041}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachi-i|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位|1=Senior Eighth Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545368}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. <!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--><!--UnRanked--> [[Category:Court Ranks of Japan]] [[Category:Ritsuryō System of Japan]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11504610}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:无位]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11504610]] Wikidata name: mui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court Rank|ja|位階|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Executive (government)|1=kanshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452091}} and held {{ill|East Asian government position|lt=official positions|ja|官職|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/East Asian government position|qq=}} but did not possess a court Rank.
== Overview == Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the correlation between official positions and court Ranks was defined by the [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier System of Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} and those not treated as {{ill|Hinkan|lt=Hinkan|lt=Pinkan|ja|品官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pinkan|qq=}}.
For example: - The [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikiji Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 職事|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|qq=}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大初位|1=Greater Initial Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433041}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachi-i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位|1=Senior Eighth Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545368}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Umemura|1=Takashi Umemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24255034}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Umemura|1=Takashi Umemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24255034}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:无位]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11504610]] Wikidata name: mui [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:16, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Mu-i''' (无位・無位, "Mu-i") refers to individuals without a {{ill|court Rank|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/court Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=court rank in Japan|WD=Q99196082}} under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]]. This term often specifically denotes those who were employed in {{ill|Executive (government)|lt=government offices|ja|官司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Executive (government)|1=kanshi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452091}} and held {{ill|East Asian government position|lt=official positions|ja|官職|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/East Asian government position|qq=}} but did not possess a court Rank.
== Overview == Under the [[:en:Ritsuryō|Ritsuryō system]], the correlation between official positions and court Ranks was defined by the [[:en:List of Japanese court ranks, positions and hereditary titles|Kan'i sōtō system]]. However, some lower-level positions, particularly those classified as {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}}, were excluded from this system. This was particularly common for positions below the {{ill|Four-Tier System of Officials|ja|四等官|1=Shitōkan|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Four-Tier System of Officials|13=User:Immanuelle/Shitōkan|qq=}} and those not treated as {{ill|Hinkan|lt=Hinkan|lt=Pinkan|ja|品官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pinkan|qq=}}.
For example: - The [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikiji Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 職事|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|qq=}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大初位|1=Greater Initial Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433041}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachi-i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位|1=Senior Eighth Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545368}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Umemura|1=Takashi Umemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24255034}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.

For example: - The [[:en:Department of Divinities|Department of Divinities]] had a total of 89 positions, but only seven were {{ill|Shikiji Officials|ja|職事官|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shikiji Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 職事|1=Shikijikan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11611227}} with corresponding Ranks. - The {{ill|City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|ja|市司|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/City Offices (Tōji and Sai)|qq=}} each had 39 positions, but only three—Chief (Sei), Assistant (Yū), and Clerk (Reishi)—were Shikiji Officials. Positions Ranked below Value Chief (Kaichō) were considered {{ill|Miscellaneous Officials|ja|雑任|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Officials|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 官人|1=kanjin|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11452075}} without corresponding Ranks.
These Miscellaneous Officials included not only individuals with low Ranks, such as {{ill|Shōi|ja|初位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shōi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大初位|1=Greater Initial Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11433041}} or {{ill|Hachi-i|ja|八位|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hachi-i|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 正八位|1=Senior Eighth Rank|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11545368}}, but also individuals entirely without Rank, referred to as "Mu-i."
References in legal texts like the [[:en:Yōrō Code|Yōrō Code]] confirm the existence of Mu-i officials. For instance: - The {{ill|Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja|衣服令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Clothing Regulations (Ifuku-ryō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} specified that Mu-i individuals were to wear yellow robes. - The {{ill|Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja|雑令|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miscellaneous Regulations (Zatsu-ryō)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} required Mu-i individuals to deliver one bundle of firewood as part of their obligations for the annual offerings on the 15th day of the New Year.
Mu-i officials were appointed to Miscellaneous Official positions through the prescribed procedures. They sought opportunities for promotion or acquisition of Rank through diligence and accumulation of merit. While some were fortunate enough to be granted Ranks through the {{ill|Merit Evaluation|ja|考課|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Merit Evaluation|qq=}} process, this opportunity was limited, and many aspects of their actual circumstances remain unclear.
== References == * {{ill|Takashi Umemura|ja|梅村喬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takashi Umemura|1=Takashi Umemura|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q24255034}}, "The Ancient Governmental System and 'Shoku'," in *The Formation Process of the 'Shoku'* (校倉書房, 2011) ISBN 978-4-7517-4360-7.
